annotations.label: Register machine

anchor.snippet: a register machine is a generic class of abstract machines

annotations.note: Mathlib's Computability folder formalizes Turing machines and partial recursive functions but contains no register machine model.

annotations.label: Sub-classes of register machines

anchor.snippet: There are several sub-classes of register machines

annotations.note: No register machine sub-class taxonomy (counter, pointer, RAM, RASP) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Registers

anchor.snippet: the model uses multiple uniquely addressed registers

annotations.note: Mathlib has no notion of addressed register storage for an abstract machine.

annotations.label: Counter machine

anchor.snippet: Counter machine – the most primitive and reduced theoretical model of computer hardware

annotations.note: Grep for 'counter machine' across Mathlib/Computability returns no hits.

annotations.label: Pointer machine

anchor.snippet: Pointer machine – a blend of the counter machine and RAM models

annotations.note: No pointer machine model is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Random-access machine (RAM)

anchor.snippet: Random-access machine (RAM) – a counter machine with indirect addressing

annotations.note: Mathlib has no random-access machine formalization.

annotations.label: Random-access stored-program machine (RASP)

anchor.snippet: Random-access stored-program machine model (RASP) – a RAM with instructions in its registers

annotations.note: Grep for 'RASP' returns nothing in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Register machines are Turing complete

anchor.snippet: Any properly defined register machine model is Turing complete

annotations.note: Without a register machine definition, Mathlib cannot state or prove their Turing completeness.

annotations.label: Formal definition of register machine

anchor.snippet: A register machine consists of

annotations.note: No structure encoding the (registers, instruction register, instruction list) tuple is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: State register (IR)

anchor.snippet: A special instruction register (IR), distinct from the registers mentioned earlier

annotations.note: No instruction register concept exists in Mathlib's computability files.

annotations.label: List of labelled instructions

anchor.snippet: List of labelled instructions, usually in sequential order

annotations.note: Mathlib has no labelled-instruction program type for a register machine.

annotations.label: Wang B-machine

anchor.snippet: his definition of his Wang B-machine

annotations.note: Grep for 'Wang' in computability files returns no machine-model hits.

annotations.label: W-machine

anchor.snippet: Lee named this a "W-machine" model

annotations.note: Lee's W-machine is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Few tapes suffice with Gödel encoding

anchor.section: Minsky, Melzak–Lambek and Shepherdson–Sturgis models "cut the tape" into many
anchor.snippet: prove that only a few tapes—as few as one—still allow the machine to be Turing equivalent

annotations.note: No Minsky-style tape-reduction theorem is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Minsky two-tape reduction

anchor.section: Minsky, Melzak–Lambek and Shepherdson–Sturgis models "cut the tape" into many
anchor.snippet: shows that the need for this bizarre instruction set can be relaxed

annotations.note: Grep for 'Minsky' across Mathlib returns nothing.

annotations.label: Melzak's pebble-and-holes model

anchor.section: Melzak's (1961) model is different: clumps of pebbles go into and out of holes
anchor.snippet: called them "holes in the ground" to be filled with "pebble counters"

annotations.note: Melzak's model is absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lambek's atomized model

anchor.section: Lambek (1961) atomizes Melzak's model into the Minsky (1961) model: INC and DEC-with-test
anchor.snippet: took Melzak's ternary model and atomized it down to the two unary instructions

annotations.note: Lambek's INC/DEC machine is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: RASP

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: A RASP or random-access stored-program machine begins as a counter machine

annotations.note: RASP has no Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Bounded counter-based RASP not Turing equivalent

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: this computer-like, von Neumann machine will not be Turing equivalent

annotations.note: Elgot–Robinson's negative result has no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Computed goto

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: Computed goto: A RASP program of instructions that modifies the "goto address"

annotations.note: No computed-goto instruction semantics appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Four-register multiplication program

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: A counter machine equipped with only four unbounded registers can e.g. multiply any two numbers

annotations.note: Mathlib lacks the underlying counter machine, so this example program is not formalized.

annotations.label: Indexed instruction example

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: effective address will be

annotations.note: No effective-address calculation example exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Indirect addressing

anchor.snippet: Indirect addressing: A pointer-register supplies the finite state machine with the address

annotations.note: Grep for 'indirect address' or 'pointer-register' across Mathlib yields nothing.

annotations.label: Unbounded pointer-register yields Turing equivalence

anchor.snippet: If the pointer-register is unbounded, the RAM, and a suitable RASP built on its chassis, will be Turing equivalent

annotations.note: Hartmanis's RAM-vs-Turing equivalence is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Counter-based RASP computes primitive recursive but not all mu-recursive functions

anchor.section: Elgot–Robinson (1964) and the problem of the RASP without indirect addressing
anchor.snippet: The counter machine based RASP can compute any primitive recursive function

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes primitive recursive and partial/mu-recursive functions (Mathlib.Computability.Primrec, Mathlib.Computability.Partrec) but lacks the counter-machine RASP needed to state this separation result.
